sections.heading: How to approach a design interview
sections.content:
1. Clarify requirements — users, traffic, latency, consistency
  2. High-level architecture — main components and data flow
  3. Deep dive — critical path, data model, scaling
  4. Trade-offs — CAP, cost, complexity
  5. Operational concerns — monitoring, deployment, failure

sections.heading: Scalability Patterns
sections.content:
• Stateless services — any instance handles any request
  • Horizontal scaling — add instances behind load balancer
  • Read replicas — distribute read load
  • Caching — Redis for hot data
  • Event-driven — decouple with Kafka
  • Partitioning — shard data by key

sections.heading: CAP Theorem
sections.content:
In a distributed system, during a network partition you must choose between:

  • Consistency — all nodes see the same data
  • Availability — every request receives a response

Most real systems choose eventual consistency with strong consistency only where business requires it (e.g., payment, inventory).

sections.heading: Saga Pattern
sections.content:
Coordinates multi-service transactions without distributed locks:

Order Created → Payment Reserved → Stock Reserved → Delivery Scheduled
     ↑                                                    |
     └──── Compensate (refund, release stock) ←──── Failure

Orchestration = central coordinator. Choreography = services react to events.

sections.heading: Transactional Outbox
sections.content:
// Same DB transaction:
BEGIN;
  INSERT INTO orders (id, status) VALUES (...);
  INSERT INTO outbox (event_type, payload) VALUES ('OrderCreated', ...);
COMMIT;

// Separate process publishes outbox → Kafka

Guarantees the event is published if and only if the business change is committed.

sections.heading: Circuit Breaker
sections.content:
CLOSED → (failures exceed threshold) → OPEN
  ↑                                        |
  └── HALF-OPEN ← (timeout expires) ←─────┘
        ↓
  (test call succeeds → CLOSED)
  (test call fails → OPEN)

Protects against cascading failures when a dependency is unavailable.

sections.heading: Three Pillars
sections.content:
• Logs — discrete events with context (structured JSON)
  • Metrics — numeric measurements over time (counters, histograms)
  • Traces — request flow across services (correlation IDs)

sections.heading: SLI / SLO / SLA
sections.content:
• SLI (indicator) — the metric: e.g., % of requests < 200ms
  • SLO (objective) — the target: e.g., 99.9% of requests < 200ms
  • SLA (agreement) — the contract with consequences

Error budget = allowed unreliability (100% - SLO target)

sections.heading: What to Monitor in Logistics
sections.content:
• Deliveries stuck in a state > N minutes
  • Routes not assigned before departure time
  • Consumer lag on critical topics
  • Duplicate assignment attempts
  • Delay between physical event and system update
  • Reconciliation queue depth

A technically healthy service can still represent a broken business process.
